title: N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor antagonists are less effective in blocking long-term potentiation at apical than basal dendrites in hippocampal CA1 of awake rats
abstract:
N-Methyl-D-aspartate Receptor Antagonists
Are Less Effective in Blocking Long-Term
Potentiation at Apical Than Basal Dendrites
in Hippocampal CA1 of Awake Rats
L. Stan Leung and Bixia Shen
Department of Physiology and Clinical Neurological
Sciences, University of Western Ontario,
London, Canada
ABSTRACT:
Long-term potentiation (LTP) of ﬁeld excitatory postsynap-
tic potentials (fEPSPs) at the apical or basal dendrites of CA1 pyramidal
cells was induced by stimulation with a 1-s train of 200-Hz pulses in awake
rats, with or without the presence of various doses of an N-methyl-D-
aspartate (NMDA) receptor antagonist. Apical LTP was blocked by an
intracerebroventricular (i.c.v.) dose of 40 mg D-2-amino-5-phosphonopen-
tanoic acid (D-AP5) or 20 mg/kg i.p. D-2-amino-4-methyl-5-phosphono-3-
pentanoic acid (CGP-40116), whereas basal LTP was blocked by half the
dose of D-AP5 or CGP-40116. The noncompetitive antagonist MK-801
(I1 mg/kg i.p.) had no signiﬁcant effect on apical LTP. Apical LTP was not
blocked by i.c.v. nifedipine. The effect of an NMDA receptor antagonist
alone on apical and basal fEPSPs was also evaluated, to assess the net effect
of the NMDA receptor antagonist in blocking LTP. MK-801 (0.5–1 mg/kg
i.p.) or CGP-40116 (10–20 mg/kg i.p.) but not D-AP5 suppressed apical
fEPSPs for several hours and confounded the expression of apical LTP
during this time. We concluded that hippocampal LTP at different synapses
has different sensitivity to NMDA receptor antagonists and that a general
blockade of hippocampal NMDA receptor functions cannot be inferred by
a single hippocampal LTP measure. Hippocampus 1999;9:617–630.
r 1999 Wiley-Liss, Inc.
KEY WORDS:
D-2-amino-5-phosphonopentanoic acid; CGP-40116; MK-
801; behavioral hyperactivity; ﬁeld potentials
INTRODUCTION
Long-term potentiation (LTP), a long-lasting enhancement of synaptic
transmission, has been widely regarded as a cellular correlate of memory.
The most common type of LTP in the CA1 and dentate gyrus of the
hippocampus requires N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors (Colling-
ridge et al., 1983; Harris et al., 1984; Larson and Lynch, 1988; Malenka
and Nicoll, 1993). During tetanic stimulation, postsynaptic neuronal
depolarization opens voltage-sensitive NMDA receptor channels, resulting
in Ca21 inﬂux and LTP (Bliss and Collingridge, 1993;
Malenka and Nicoll, 1993).
Recent studies have shown the diversity of LTP in the
hippocampus. A non-NMDA–dependent type of LTP
was found, notably at the mossy ﬁber to CA3 (Zalutsky
and Nicoll, 1990) and the lateral perforant path to
dentate gyrus (Bramham et al., 1991) synapses. Under
some conditions, non-NMDA receptor–dependent LTP
was also found in CA1 (Aniksztejn and Ben-Ari, 1991;
Hanse and Gustafsson, 1994). Grover and Teyler (1990,
1994) showed that the LTP induced by a high-frequency
(HF) 200-Hz stimulation in vitro was resistant to an
NMDA receptor antagonists, in contrast to LTP in-
duced by theta-frequency primed bursts, which was
blocked by NMDA receptor antagonist (Larson and
Lynch, 1988; Leung and Au, 1994). In addition, LTP at
different synapses may vary in its optimal induction
procedure or magnitude, as shown for the apical vs. the
basal dendritic LTP in CA1 in vivo (Leung et al., 1992;
Kaibara and Leung, 1993; Leung and Shen, 1995).
The importance of studying LTP in awake, unanesthe-
tized rats cannot be overemphasized. To elucidate the
participation of LTP in behavioral functions, the proper-
ties of LTP in awake rats must be studied. So far, most of
the studies on hippocampal LTP in awake rats were
focused on a single synapse of the medial perforant path
in the dentate gyrus (Morris et al., 1986; Davis et al.,
1992; Barnes et al., 1994; Saucier and Cain, 1995).
Studies in vitro (Harris et al., 1984; Larson and
Lynch, 1988; Leung and Au, 1994) and in anesthetized
rats (Abraham et al., 1988; Roth and Leung, 1995) have
generally indicated that the LTP at the CA3 to CA1
synapses is NMDA receptor dependent. However, pre-
liminary data suggested a different conclusion in awake
rats, in which LTP at the apical dendritic synapse in CA1
after 200-Hz high-frequency (HF) stimulation was
resistant to intracerebroventricular (i.c.v.) pretreatment
with
D,L-2-amino-5-phosphonopentanoic acid (D,L-
AP5) (Leung and Shen, 1992). Therefore, we hypoth-
esized that the apical LTP in CA1 of awake rats was

Previous results indicate that the basal LTP induced by theta-
frequency primed bursts was sensitive to D,L-AP5 (Leung and
Shen, 1993). Because apical LTP was relatively difficult to elicit
with theta-frequency primed bursts (Leung and Shen, 1995), a
200-Hz HF tetanus (Leung and Shen, 1995) was used to induce
apical or basal LTP in this study. LTP in the awake rat was studied
after various doses of an NMDA receptor antagonist, including
the competitive antagonists D-AP5 (i.c.v.) and CGP-40116 (i.p.)
and the noncompetitive antagonist MK-801 (i.p.).
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Electrode Placement, Stimulation and Recording
Paradigms
Under sodium pentobarbital anesthesia, male hooded rats
(250–400 g) were implanted bilaterally with 125-mm wire
electrodes in the dorsal hippocampus. The electrodes were
Teﬂon-insulated except at the cut tips, and were used for either
stimulation or recording. Recording electrodes were implanted in
CA1 (P3 to 4.5 according to atlas by Paxinos and Watson, 1986),
straddling the CA1 cell layer of either side (Fig. 1; Leung and
Shen, 1995). The surface recording electrode was typically at a
depth from the white matter of the neocortex to CA1 stratum
oriens, and the deep recording electrode was in CA1 stratum
radiatum. Stimulating electrodes were placed anterior to the
recording electrodes (at about P2 to P3.5; Fig. 1). Two screws in
the skull over the frontal cortex and cerebellum served, respec-
tively, as the stimulus anode and the recording ground. Photo-
isolated cathodal current pulses were used for stimulation. A
23-gauge cannula was placed above the lateral ventricle (P0.8,
L1.4) for subsequent intracerebroventricular (i.c.v.) injections. At
the end of the experiments, the electrode and cannula placements
were veriﬁed in thionin-stained sections of the brain.
Two groups of rats were used, one intended for apical dendritic
LTP and another for basal dendritic LTP. In the apical group, the
stimulating electrode was targeted at stratum radiatum (apical
dendritic layer) of CA1 (S1 in Fig. 1). Stimulation of the stratum
radiatum evoked a ﬁeld excitatory postsynaptic potential (fEPSP),
which was negative in the apical dendritic layers and positive in
stratum oriens and the alveus (Fig. 1; Leung and Shen, 1995). In
the basal group, the stimulating electrode was targeted at the
stratum oriens (basal dendritic layer) of CA1 (S2 in Fig. 1).
Stimulation of the stratum oriens evoked an fEPSP that was
negative at the basal dendrites and positive at the apical dendrites
(Fig. 1; Leung and Shen, 1995). The stimulus and recording
electrodes were optimized during implantation by using estab-
lished electrophysiologic criteria (Leung, 1979). Anatomical data
indicated that CA3 projected to the strata radiatum and oriens of
both sides (Swanson et al., 1978); thus, either ipsilateral or
contralateral stimulation that evoked a clear basal or apical
dendritic response was used (Leung et al., 1992; Leung and Shen,
1995). In rats in which both apical and basal dendritic responses
in CA1 could be elicited (they were included as apical OR basal
groups, depending on the afferent that was tetanized), low-
intensity paired-pulses applied to the same stimulating electrode
(homosynaptic) showed paired-pulse facilitation, whereas hetero-
synaptic (basal then apical stimulation or apical then basal
stimulation) showed no paired-pulse facilitation (Kaibara and
Leung, 1993), suggesting that the stimulated afferents did not
overlap. Heterosynaptic LTP was only elicited infrequently (Leung
and Shen, 1995).
LTP in this study was evoked by a 1-s stimulation train of 200
Hz, which we called an HF train or tetanus (Leung and Shen,
1995). Hippocampal CA3 neurons ﬁred at high frequencies for up
to 100 ms during normal sharp waves in the EEG (Buzsaki et al.,
1983; Buzsaki, 1989), thus giving credence to the use of HF
stimulations (Leung and Shen, 1995). In each rat, the threshold
intensity of one afferent was deﬁned as the minimal stimulus
intensity that evoked a visually detectable response. An HF train
was delivered during awake immobility, with hippocampal EEG
being continuously recorded. Typically, in each rat, the minimal
HF intensity that induced LTP of .20% enhancement was
determined, starting at a tetanus intensity of 1.23 threshold
intensity, and not exceeding 23 threshold intensity. Tetanus that
evoked an afterdischarge (AD) of .15-s duration was shown to
reduce LTP magnitude, and these experiments were not included
for analysis. However, experiments in which LTP, basal or apical,
was induced after a short (,15 s) AD were included, because the
LTP magnitudes were comparable to experiments that evoked no
AD (Leung and Shen, 1993, 1995). In some rats, apical LTP
could not be evoked by an HF train intensity below the AD
threshold (Leung and Shen, 1995), and these rats were excluded.
In every rat with veriﬁed basal dendritic stimulating electrodes,
basal LTP could always be induced by an HF train.
Recording was performed between 0900 and 1800 hours. The
ﬁrst experiment started 1 week after surgery, after the rat was
habituated to the recording setup. Single test pulses (0.1-ms pulse
duration and 10–400 mA) were delivered to either the basal or the
apical dendritic afferents, and responses from two to three
electrodes were averaged (eight sweeps) on line by using a custom
program (sampling rate, 7–10 KHz) during awake immobility.
Awake immobility was operationally deﬁned as when the rat held
its head up against gravity, with eyes open and no gross body
movements. Behavioral control (or behavioral clamp) was neces-
sary because the apical dendritic fEPSPs in CA1 varied with
behavior (Leung, 1980). During each recording session, the rat
was connected to a cable and left alone until it became spontane-
ously immobile. The time-dependence of the average fEPSPs was
assessed using 1.5–23 threshold intensity test pulses, for at least
60 min before a drug/control injection, at various times after a
drug/control injection, and at 10 ﬁxed times after a HF tetanus,
namely 5, 10, 20, 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, 180 min, and 1 day. An
input-output series of the fEPSPs, using 1.2, 1.5, 2, and 33
threshold stimulus intensity, was determined before drug/control
injection, and at 1 h, 2 h, and 1 day after the tetanus.
Each rat was used in paired drug/control experiments, in a
counterbalanced temporal order, with .4 days between experi-
ments. The tetanic stimulus intensity was kept the same across

NMDA Receptor Antagonists and Other
The NMDA receptor antagonist D,L-AP5 (Sigma, St. Louis,
MO) or D-AP5 (Research Biochemical International, Natick,
MA) was dissolved in 0.9% saline. Nifedipine was dissolved in
dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and protected from light. Saline,
D,L-AP5, D-AP5, nifedipine, or DMSO was injected i.c.v. 15
min before a tetanus. A Hamilton syringe was used to inject the
drug/vehicle through a 30-gauge inner cannula that protruded 1
mm beyond the implanted outer cannula over the lateral ventricle
(Leung and Desborough, 1988); a volume of #4 ml was given
CGP-40116 (D-2-amino-4-methyl-5-phosphono-3-pentanoic
acid), a gift from Novartis, Basel, Switzerland, is a competitive
NMDA receptor antagonist chemically related to D-AP5, but it
crosses the blood-brain barrier (Fagg et al., 1990). Noncompeti-
tive NMDA receptor antagonist MK-801 (Research Biochemical
International) or CGP-40116 was dissolved in 0.9% saline.
Unless otherwise speciﬁed, MK-801 (0.5 mg/kg i.p.) or CGP-
40116 (10 mg/kg i.p.) was given at 30 min before the tetanus,
MK-801 (1 mg/kg i.p.) at 1 h before, and CGP-40116 (20 mg/kg
i.p.) at 2 h before the tetanus. Experiments that used drug-alone
injection indicated that the times of injection used would give
near-maximal drug effect as judged by the induced behavioral
hyperactivity (Hargreaves and Cain, 1992; Lapin and Rogawski,
1995; Cain et al., 1996). Because behavioral activity correlated
with a smaller apical fEPSP (Leung, 1980), hyperactivity after 1
mg/kg i.p. MK-801 or 20 mg/kg i.p. CGP-40116 was suppressed
by injection of 0.5 mg/kg i.p. haloperidol (Lapin and Rogawski,
1995) at 15 min before the tetanus. If used with an NMDA
receptor antagonist, haloperidol was also given in the paired
control experiments. Pilot studies indicated that haloperidol had
no signiﬁcant effect on apical LTP.
The slope (over 2- to 4-ms interval) on the rising phase of the
fEPSP and the peak of the fEPSP were measured. Unless
otherwise stated, the fEPSP measures reported here were derived
from the electrode at CA1 stratum radiatum (deep electrode).
This was because the deep electrode typically recorded a larger
signal than the surface electrode. Also, our technique of electrode
placement allowed precise location of the deep electrode in the
apical dendritic layer of CA1, but less precise placement of the
surface electrode, which was typically found in a location ranging
from CA1 stratum oriens to the deep neocortex. Recording the
negative apical response directly at the synaptic layer is the natural
choice, but recording the positive basal response remote from the
activated synapse (i.e., in the apical dendritic layer) may appear
unusual. However, both positive and negative responses evoked by
the basal (or apical) dendritic afferents (Fig. 1) were generated by
the same synaptic event or they correspond to the two ‘‘poles’’ of a
dipole ﬁeld generated by CA1 pyramidal cells (Leung, 1979; Roth
and Leung, 1995). In previous studies (Leung et al., 1992; Leung
and Shen, 1995), it was shown that LTP at different electrodes was
comparable, which was also conﬁrmed in this study (see, e.g.,
Table 1). It should also be noted that the dentate gyrus ﬁeld
responses in vivo were typically derived from the hilus of dentate
gyrus where the perforant-path evoked fEPSP was positive-going
(Morris, 1989; Gilbert and Mack, 1990; Davis et al., 1992; Barnes
et al., 1994; Cain et al., 1996).
Drug effects on the fEPSP measures after a HF tetanus were
assessed by a two-factor (group 3 time) repeated measure analysis
of variance (ANOVA) followed by post hoc Tukey’s t-tests. The
responses were derived from a drug-injected experiment and its
paired control experiment (two groups), recorded at each of the 10
ﬁxed times (5 min to day 1, described above). For each
experiment, a grand average of all the fEPSP measures recorded
before the drug and tetanus was used as the baseline value (100%),
and the response after tetanus was normalized by this grand
baseline average. Potentiation at ﬁxed times (1 h, 2 h, and 1 day)

Control for Direct Drug Effects
To control for the direct drug action on the evoked fEPSPs,
drug injections were given without delivery of a tetanus and
fEPSPs were recorded at the same ﬁxed times as after a tetanus. We
will call this the ‘‘drug-alone’’ control. Some researchers preferred
to change the stimulus intensity to restore the test responses to the
baseline before drug, which we will call the ‘‘response-restoration’’
method. The response-restoration method does not compensate
for time-dependent changes of the fEPSPs induced by a drug. For
example, a drug may decrease fEPSPs for only 1 h, and if a tetanus
is delivered within this time, the response-restoration method will
overcompensate when the drug effect has dissipated (e.g., more
than 1 h after drug), resulting in false potentiation. On the other
hand, if the effect of the drug alone was to decrease the fEPSPs
more after than before the tetanus, the ‘‘response-restoration’’
method will undercompensate, resulting in a false suppression of
potentiation after the tetanus. The drug-alone control method is
not without problems, because the same drug may not yield the
same time course of changes in two different experiments.
However, as long as the latter variability was random and not
related to irreversible effects such as drug tolerance and cell death,
repeated experiments should reduce the statistical variability. If the
drug-alone control indicated a statistically signiﬁcant drug effect,
the following compensation of the group mean was done: at each
time after the tetanus, the slope or peak measure of the fEPSP of
each experiment (already expressed as a percentage of the baseline
before drug injection) was divided by the mean measure after drug
alone (also expressed as a percentage of the baseline) for a group of
rats. This compensation assumes that the percentage reduction (or
enhancement) of the fEPSP at a ﬁxed time after a drug was the
same with or without tetanus. Alternatively, to allow for compen-
sation in each individual experiment, a different ‘‘individual
compensation’’ procedure may be used if the mean drug effect
remained fairly constant for a period of time. For each drug, the
period of time during which the direct drug effect was constant
(e.g., for 1 h after 10 mg/kg i.p. CGP-40116) was determined,
and the fEPSP measure at 5–10 min before the tetanus was used
to normalize the fEPSP measures obtained within the period of
constant drug effect. The individual compensation method is
similar to the response-restoration method above in assuming a
period of constant effect of a drug alone. The latter period was
empirically determined in this study.
Apical Dendritic LTP and Effect of D-AP5
Stimulation of the stratum radiatum evoked negative fEPSPs at
the stratum radiatum recording electrode (L1 in Fig. 1A) and
positive responses at the surface electrode (L2 in Fig. 1A). In 47
pairs of main experiments summarized below, the threshold
intensity for evoking an apical response was 36 6 2 mA
(mean 6 standard error of the mean [SEM]), and the tetanic
stimulus intensity was 53 6 3 mA (range, 30–100 mA; n 5 47).
At 1.53 threshold, the test stimulus evoked an apical fEPSP slope
at the deep recording electrode of 0.28 6 0.04 mV/ms. After a
HF tetanus of the CA1 stratum radiatum on one side, apical LTP
was typically found in CA1 on both sides. Apical LTP was
typically manifested as a 20–40% increase of the baseline slope or
peak measure, for more than 3 h after the tetanus (Fig. 2A). In
most rats, apical LTP of 10–30% magnitude was still observed on
1 day after the tetanus (Fig. 3A).
The behavioral effects of i.c.v. D-AP5 (and D,L-AP5) were
similar for all doses used. Hind-limb weakness and ataxia during
walking were observed with the lowest dose of AP5. Loss of
postural balance was observed for a short time period (typically
,10 min) after the highest dose (D-AP5 40 mg i.c.v.). Severe
hyperactivity were not usually observed. The direct effect of
D-AP5 and D,L-AP5 on the apical fEPSPs was not signiﬁcant
with any of the dose used (dotted lines in Fig. 3). The estimated
ventricular concentration of 20 mg D-AP5 was 68 mM, assuming
uniform distribution over 1.5-ml volume of cerebrospinal ﬂuid.
Apical LTP after 20 mg i.c.v. D-AP5 was not similar to that
after i.c.v. saline. In Figure 2B, after pretreatment of 20 mg i.c.v.
D-AP5, a HF train induced similar or larger fEPSPs compared
with those in the saline i.c.v. experiment (Fig. 2A). In a group of
four rats (Fig. 3B), repeated-measures ANOVA gave no signiﬁcant
group, time, or group 3 time effect (P . .64). Input-output

Apical LTP was also studied after a dose of 20 mg DL-AP5
i.c.v., which was sufficient to block the primed-burst–induced
basal LTP of awake rats (Leung and Shen, 1993). Interestingly,
DL-AP5 (20 mg i.c.v.) increased the mean apical LTP (Fig. 3A).
Repeated-measures ANOVA revealed no group effect (F(1,
10) 5 0.44, P 5 .52), but the time (F(9, 90) 5 3.87, P , .001)
and the group 3 time (F(9, 90) 5 2.21, P , .03) effects were

larger (P , .05) in the D,L-AP5 than control group at 60, 90,
150, and 180 min after the tetanus (Fig. 3A).

Effect of MK-801 on Evoked Apical Responses
Apical fEPSPs and LTP were evaluated after 1 mg/kg i.p.
MK-801. MK-801 suppressed the apical fEPSPs by .20% for
more than 3 h after injection (Fig. 8B), and this effect was only
partly alleviated by haloperidol. In the example shown (Fig. 8A),
an increase in the slope or peak of the apical fEPSP (above the
predrug or pretetanus baseline) was clearly induced by a HF train
under the inﬂuence of 1 mg/kg i.p. MK-801. When compared
with LTP after control injections (data not shown), apical LTP
after 1 mg/kg i.p. MK-801 was somewhat smaller. However, the
smaller apical fEPSPs after MK-801 could be partly attributed to a
direct drug effect. When the LTP measures were compensated by
either the drug-alone effect (Materials and Methods section), the
effect of MK-801 on apical LTP was marginally nonsigniﬁcant
(.05 , P , .1), as shown by repeated-measures ANOVA; the
group 3 time interaction effect was not signiﬁcant (P . .4).

ized by the pretetanus measure, for up to 90 min after tetanus

Apical LTP Is Relatively Resistant to NMDA
Apical LTP was found to be more resistant than basal LTP to
NMDA receptor antagonists. D-AP5 (10–20 mg i.c.v.) and

unpublished data). Thus, the difference in sensitivity to an

The conclusion about the relative resistance of the apical LTP
to NMDA receptor antagonist does not depend on the compensa-
tion procedures. No compensation procedure was applied with
the use of DL-AP5 or D-AP5. The compensation applied after
CGP-40116 and MK-801 serves to reveal the time course of LTP
during the period of direct drug action. Even after the direct drug
effects had dissipated, as on 1 day after the tetanus, basal but not
apical LTP was blocked by 20 mg i.c.v. D-AP5 and 10 mg/kg i.p.
Why Is Apical LTP in Awake Rats Resistant to
The D-AP5 dose response of the apical LTP in awake rats,
without inhibition blockade, was similar to that shown by Hanse
and Gustafsson (1995) in vitro, in the presence of GABAA channel
blocker picrotoxin. We and Hanse and Gustafsson (1995), found
that apical LTP induced by a HF train was enhanced by a low dose
and blocked by a high dose of D-AP5. Partial blockade of
feed-forward inhibition (Sah et al., 1990) may be responsible for
the increased LTP at a low dose of D-AP5.
We infer that the apical LTP was NMDA receptor dependent,
because it was blocked by a high dose of D-AP5 or CGP-40116
(Fig. 12). NMDA receptors in the brain are heterogeneous, and
receptors with different subunits may have different affinity for
glutamate and NMDA receptor antagonists (Monaghan et al.,
1988; Monyer et al., 1994; McBain and Mayer, 1994). We have
no direct evidence that NMDA receptors at the basal and apical
synapses are different. Davis et al. (1992) suggested that D-AP5
may be trapped to the extracellular membrane by a Ca21-
dependent mechanism, and it is possible that this trapping may
differ between apical and basal dendrites, thus affecting the
effective D-AP5 local concentration. No experimental evidence is
available for the above speculation. More importantly, the time
course of glutamate at the synapse (Clements et al., 1992) and the
kinetics of action of competitive antagonists (Mayer et al., 1994)
would affect the magnitude of postsynaptic NMDA currents. At
the synaptic cleft of hippocampal cultured neurons, a single
presynaptic action potential was inferred to release glutamate that
peaked at 1.1 mM and decayed in 1.2 ms (Clements et al., 1992).
The concentration and time course of synaptic glutamate in vivo
are not known, and these measures may vary at different
hippocampal synapses in the awake rat. Because of a strong
diffusion barrier (McBain et al., 1990) and glutamate spillover
from neighboring synapses (Barbour and Hausser, 1997), tetanic
(HF) stimulation of the stratum radiatum in awake rats may result
in a prolonged presence of glutamate (of millimolar concentra-
tion) in the synaptic cleft, which may displace the competitive
antagonist (likely in micromolar concentration) from the NMDA
receptors. The D-AP5 dissociation rate constant of 19.4 s21 (at
24–277C; Benveniste and Mayer, 1991) suggests that a glutamate
pulse of .50-ms duration may displace D-AP5 from the NMDA
receptor (a 19-fold increase in duration is needed to displace
CGP-40116, which has a 1.1 s21 dissociation constant). Tetanic
stimulations in vitro may be expected to release less synaptic
glutamate for two reasons: (1) the convergent glutamatergic
collaterals to CA1 are severed (Swanson et al., 1978), and (2) the
use of inhibition blockers and the cutting of GABAergic axonal
collaterals (Schwartzkroin, 1981) permit LTP at lower tetanic
stimulus intensities, evoking less glutamate release in vitro.
We do not have any evidence of an NMDA receptor-
independent mechanism for apical LTP in awake rats. Glutamate
metabotropic receptors apparently do not mediate LTP when
NMDA channels are blocked (Bortolotto et al., 1994; Riedel et
al., 1995). The Ca21 L-channel antagonist nifedipine did not
block apical LTP in awake rats. Hanse and Gustafsson (1995)
showed a similar result in vitro. However, Grover and Teyler
(1990, 1994) reported that apical LTP induced by a 200-Hz HF
train in vitro was blocked by nifedipine but not by AP5. The role
played by high-threshold Ca21 channels has not been studied.
High-threshold Ca21 channels contribute to apical dendritic Ca21
inﬂux during spiking (Christie et al., 1995) or normal synaptic
activation (Miyakawa et al., 1992; Magee et al., 1995). Because
apical LTP appears to be suppressed by a high dose of an NMDA
receptor antagonist or a more complete blockade of the NMDA
channels, we may speculate that a synergistic opening of both
NMDA and high-threshold Ca21 channels contribute to the
critical amount of intracellular Ca21 required for LTP.
Expression of LTP Depends on Direct or Indirect
The noncompetitive NMDA receptor antagonist MK-801
should block NMDA receptors, independent of glutamate concen-
tration. However, MK-801 at #1 mg/kg i.p., which already
induced toxic behavioral effects in rats (see also Hargreaves and
Cain, 1992), did not signiﬁcantly block apical or basal LTP
induced by a HF train in CA1. No attempts were made to restore
the apical EPSPs to the baseline values after MK-801 because the
HF train already evoked LTP of normal amplitudes despite the
diminished EPSPs (Fig. 8A). Perhaps the 1-s LTP-inducing
tetanus was too brief for effective open channel blockade (Coan et
Systemic NMDA receptor antagonists were found to suppress
apical fEPSPs by several mechanisms. First, the NMDA receptor
antagonist induced behavioral hyperactivity, which may suppress
apical fEPSPs (Leung, 1980). In part, an NMDA receptor
antagonist may suppress apical fEPSPs by changing the subcorti-
cal modulation of the hippocampus, e.g., acetylcholine and
GABA release from septal afferents (Leung, 1980; Leung and
Desborough, 1988). ‘‘Behavioral clamp’’ (selective recordings
during a ﬁxed behavior of immobility), sometimes with the use of
haloperidol, alleviated this problem. Second, a direct drug effect of
MK-801 and CGP-40116 on the apical fEPSPs was inferred (Figs.
7B, 8B). A direct CGP-40116 effect on basal fEPSPs was not
found, suggesting that this was not a general effect on non-
NMDA receptors. NMDA receptors may participate in the
generation of the normal apical fEPSPs, similar to the fEPSPs in

the dentate gyrus after medial perforant path stimulation (Maren
et al., 1992, but see Errington et al., 1987; and Sah et al., 1990).
In this study, as in a previous one (Leung and Shen, 1993), we
advocate the use of drug-alone injections as an additional control.
The recording of fEPSPs after drug-alone injection revealed the
direct effect of the drug, and by compensating for this direct drug
effect, the net effect of the drug on apical LTP may be revealed.
Relevance to Behavioral Experiments
This study shows that hippocampal LTP at different synapses is
not uniform in its response to NMDA receptor antagonists. In
particular, we showed that apical and basal LTP in CA1 was
blocked by different doses of an NMDA receptor antagonist. LTP
at the medial perforant path to the dentate gyrus synapse also
appears to have a different sensitivity to NMDA receptor
antagonist compared with apical LTP in CA1. Gilbert and Mack
(1990) reported that MK-801 (at 0.1 or 1 mg/kg i.p. given 30 min
before a tetanus) blocked the LTP of the population spike in the
dentate gyrus, recorded at 24 h after tetanus in awake rats. A
similar dose of MK-801 did not signiﬁcantly affect apical LTP.
Our results have implications for many behavioral studies in
which NMDA receptor antagonists were used to block LTP. In
studies that used different injection regimens and NMDA recep-
tor antagonists, it was concluded that the blockade of the medial
perforant path to dentate LTP may block spatial learning (Morris
et al., 1986; Morris, 1989; Davis et al., 1992; but see Saucier and
Cain, 1995). It may be suggested that apical LTP in CA1 may not
have been blocked in these studies, although the speciﬁc NMDA
receptor antagonist and drug administration used may differ from
those in the present studies. There are many issues concerning
whether an NMDA-receptor–dependent LTP is a mechanism of
learning (Leung and Desborough, 1988; Keith and Rudy, 1990;
Davis et al., 1992; Saucier and Cain, 1995; Martinez and Derrick,
1996). However, it is possible that the preservation of some types
of learning (Bannerman et al., 1995; Saucier and Cain, 1995) may
be attributed to the preservation of LTP in CA1. Other studies
have implicated the importance of LTP in CA1, rather than that
in the DG, in spatial learning (Nosten-Bertrand et al., 1996; Tsien
et al., 1996; Lu et al., 1997; Wilson and Tonegawa, 1997).
An NMDA receptor antagonist induced multiple effects in the
hippocampus. A low dose (#10 mg i.c.v. D-AP5 or #0.5 mg/kg
i.p. MK-801) that did not affect apical LTP was found to shorten
the hippocampal AD duration (Leung and Shen, 1993) and
disrupt the hippocampal theta rhythm (Leung and Desborough,
1988). Pronounced sensorimotor disturbances typically accompa-
nied the blockade of apical LTP in this study, although it is not
possible to state that all NMDA receptor antagonists would block
apical LTP and sensorimotor functions equally well. However, it
seems fair to state that different events in the brain may be blocked
by different doses of an NMDA receptor antagonist. A general
blockade of hippocampal NMDA receptor functions cannot be
inferred based on the blockade of a speciﬁc event like ischemia-
induced cell death (Gutnikov and Gaffan, 1996) or LTP in the
